content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6

## Summary

All four amendments identify verified, real defects. The DefaultHasher instability and missing migration path are genuine robustness gaps in the legacy hash compatibility layer. The prompt_uses_contract() logic conflates opt-in detection with partial shape validation, creating a hole where malformed contract prompts escape enforcement. The blank-line item splitting is a straightforward parsing bug that corrupts multi-paragraph Markdown list items.

## Votes

- `fr-17-fda77f4c`: Accept
  Verified: `legacy_prompt_hash` at fs.rs:258-265 uses `std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher`, whose algorithm Rust docs explicitly say is not portable or stable across versions. The pinned test value `5b624debc08968f4` at fs.rs:4383-4386 catches compiler-driven changes during CI but cannot protect already-persisted hashes in project snapshots written under a previous compiler. Since this hash is used for persisted resume compatibility (drift.rs:150), a Rust toolchain upgrade could silently break all in-flight projects. No fixed-algorithm hasher (e.g., siphasher) is currently in Cargo.toml. Switching to an explicit, version-pinned algorithm is the correct fix for a persisted compatibility format.
- `fr-17-9e3ee6c4`: Accept
  Verified: On the `NoChange` return path (drift.rs:64-68), the SHA-256 `current_prompt_hash` is returned but never persisted. In engine.rs:1359-1364, the `NoChange` branch preserves the old baseline via `prompt_change_baseline.clone()`, so `ActiveRun.prompt_hash_at_cycle_start` retains the legacy hash. `sync_project_prompt_hash()` is only called on `Continue` and `RestartCycle` paths, never on `NoChange`. This creates a durable gap: projects that resume without prompt changes will recompute the legacy hash on every resume indefinitely, never converging to SHA-256. A one-time migration when the legacy fallback fires on the `NoChange` path is a low-risk improvement that eliminates this perpetual fallback.
- `fr-17-ee204196`: Accept
  Verified: `prompt_uses_contract()` at task_prompt_contract.rs:127-148 requires `section_index <= 1` after the marker, meaning only `## Milestone Summary` (index 0) or `## Current Bead Details` (index 1) trigger a `true` return. If a canonical prompt loses those early headings but retains the marker and later canonical sections, the function returns `false`. The sole production caller at prompt_review.rs:321 (`canonical_contract_drift_concerns`) short-circuits with an empty vector when the function returns `false`, bypassing `validate_canonical_prompt_shape()` entirely. This is exactly the scenario where validation is most needed. Using the marker alone as the opt-in signal and delegating structural checks to `validate_canonical_prompt_shape()` is the correct separation of concerns.
- `fr-17-40ebf746`: Accept
  Verified: `collect_section_items` at service.rs:586-593 unconditionally finalizes the current item buffer on any blank line (`items.push(current.trim().to_owned()); current.clear(); continue;`) without look-ahead for indented continuation lines. In standard Markdown, a list item may contain a blank line followed by an indented paragraph that belongs to the same item. The current code splits such items into separate bullets, corrupting user-authored content in the generated canonical prompt. No existing test covers multi-paragraph list item continuations. This is a clear parsing bug with a well-defined fix: peek at the next line after a blank line and, if it has leading indentation consistent with a continuation, keep it in the current item buffer.
